title: A Tiny Brain Implant That Sends Signals Without Wires
summary: Scientists at Cornell University made a very small brain implant that is tinier than a grain of salt. This tiny device can sit on a grain of salt and send brain activity information from a living animal without using wires. It works for more than a year. The implant uses light to send the brain signals. It has a special part called a semiconductor diode that catches light to power it and sends light to share data. The device is about 300 microns long and 70 microns wide, which is super tiny. This could help doctors monitor brains better in the future, like during MRI scans, and might be used in other body parts too. It is called a microscale optoelectronic tetherless electrode, or MOTE for short.

background_read: Brain implants are tiny devices that doctors put in the brain to help understand how it works. They can send signals to show what the brain is doing, like when you think or move. Scientists are making them smaller and better so they can help people with brain problems or learn more about the brain. This new implant is very small and uses light, which is safe and doesn't need wires, making it easier to use for a long time.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists at Cornell University and other researchers
WHAT: Made a tiny brain implant smaller than a grain of salt that sends brain activity data wirelessly using light
WHERE: Developed at Cornell University and Nanyang Technological University
WHY: To help monitor brain activity better for medical uses like MRI scans and future body monitoring

perspectives.description: The implant could improve brain monitoring for patients, making treatments safer and more effective.
